voiceover: In this demo, let us look at dow using a chatbot project. When you keep changing prompts and settings, code history alone is not enough. You also need to know how the chatbot's behavior changed.

We start with dow init. This gives you the basic project structure: a spec file for the chatbot behavior, and an evaluator file for custom quality checks. From this point on, dow can track behavior as versions.

screen: CLI: commit baseline and improved chatbot versions
voiceover: Now we commit a few chatbot versions. One is the baseline, then we improve the prompt, mark a golden version, try a high-temperature stress test, and branch a deterministic release. Each commit captures the outputs, settings, stability, and metrics together.

screen: Dashboard: Behavior Dashboard and Version Tree
voiceover: You can also use the dashboard to inspect the same history visually. The Version Tree shows how the chatbot moved from the baseline to the golden version, where the bad stress test happened, and where the release branch was created.

When you click into a version, dow shows the full record behind it. You can review the configuration, sampled chatbot replies, the stability score, and the service checklist metrics that tell you whether this version is actually good.

The Compare view is useful when you want to answer, what changed between these two versions? Here you can see the config difference, the semantic drift score, the stability change, and the verdict in one place.

The dashboard is not only for viewing. You can also edit the spec and capture a new version directly from the browser, so someone reviewing the chatbot does not have to leave the UI to try a small change.

Back in the CLI, you can run the same workflow in a scriptable way. dow eval scores the committed version, and dow eval draft lets you preview a change before you commit it. That makes experimentation safer.
For deeper analysis, history gives you the captured versions, inspect opens the complete record for a chosen version, and compare shows how far behavior moved from the baseline to the golden version.

Then explain tells you which field caused the drift, as long as the comparison is clean. Finally, tree shows the lineage in the terminal, and you can export it as Mermaid for documentation or a pull request.

That is the core idea of dow: your chatbot team gets a complete behavior history. You can use the dashboard for review, the CLI for automation, and the metrics and drift analysis to decide what is safe to release.
